content: The repository layer is responsible for abstracting data access and persistence in the application. It defines interfaces for interacting with data sources (such as databases, in-memory stores, or external services) and provides concrete implementations for each backend.
Purpose: Decouple domain logic from data storage and retrieval concerns.
Purpose: Enable easy swapping of data sources (e.g., in-memory, database, mock).
Purpose: Facilitate testing by allowing the use of fake or mock repositories.
Purpose: Centralize all data access logic for maintainability and consistency.
Every repository MUST define an interface for its operations (e.g., UserRepository, TenantRepository).
This enables abstraction, testability, and future extensibility.
Concrete implementations (e.g., in-memory, database) should satisfy the interface.
Naming convention: Use EntityRepository for interfaces (e.g., UserRepository), and EntityPersistence or EntityRepositoryPostgres for implementations (e.g., UserPersistence, UserRepositoryPostgres).
Interface placement: Define repository interfaces in the internal/repository package for consistency and discoverability.
Mocks: Place all repository mocks in a shared testmock package for reusability across tests.
Error handling: Repository methods should return domain-meaningful errors, not just raw database errors, to keep the domain layer clean.
Extensibility: Add new data sources (e.g., cache, external API) as new implementations of the same interface.
What Belongs Here: Repository interfaces for each aggregate/entity (e.g., UserRepository, TenantRepository).
What Belongs Here: Concrete implementations (e.g., UserPersistence, UserRepositoryPostgres).
What Belongs Here: Data mapping and transformation logic between domain models and storage representations.
Note: The repository layer is a key part of Clean Architecture, ensuring that domain logic remains independent of infrastructure and data storage details.
